EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system preceded by a deep and moist southwest flow brought a prolonged period of winter weather and widespread heavy snow to eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occurred while wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The next in a series of cold and wet storms brought another round of snow to much of Utah, with southern Utah once again receiving the heaviest snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowbasin Mid-Bowl received 13 inches of new snow, with gusty winds blowing through much of the event. The strongest winds occurred at the Sherwood Hills sensor, with a maximum wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold Pacific storm brought widespread snowfall and strong gusty winds to much of northern Utah on December 19th, with snow lingering across the higher terrain for the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowbird received a storm total of 9 inches of new snow in windy conditions, with the maximum wind gust along the Central Wasatch Peaks reaching 81 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold Pacific storm brought widespread snowfall and strong gusty winds to much of northern Utah on December 19th, with snow lingering across the higher terrain for the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Logan received 7 inches of new snow over a period of 10 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold Pacific storm brought widespread snowfall and strong gusty winds to much of northern Utah on December 19th, with snow lingering across the higher terrain for the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and moist storm system brought showers and thunderstorms to much of Utah, as well as creating strong winds across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vernon Hill sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and moist storm system brought showers and thunderstorms to much of Utah, as well as creating strong winds across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sensors in the west deserts recorded strong wind gusts, including 62 mph at Wendover Peak and 58 mph at the English Village sensor in the Dugway mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous early season storm moved through the area, producing widespread precipitation. After the initial cold front on the 3rd and 4th, lake effect snow continued into November 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowbasin received 18 inches of new snow over a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous early season storm moved through the area, producing widespread precipitation. After the initial cold front on the 3rd and 4th, lake effect snow continued into November 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alta received a storm total of 46 inches of new snow, while Snowbird and Brighton received 41 and 27 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving just north and east of Utah brought strong winds to northern Utah, particularly along the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many strong gusts were recorded along the Central Wasatch Peaks, with the highest gust being 111 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold Pacific storm brought widespread snowfall and strong gusty winds to much of northern Utah on December 19th, with snow lingering across the higher terrain for the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tooele received 8 inches of new snow for the event. Most of this snow fell on the afternoon of the 19th, with lake effect snow lingering into the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold Pacific storm brought widespread snowfall and strong gusty winds to much of northern Utah on December 19th, with snow lingering across the higher terrain for the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eden received a storm total of 10 new inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche at Logan Peak on the morning of December 24th killed two snowmobilers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Christmas Eve morning, the first clear day after several consecutive days under stormy weather conditions, four experienced snowmobilers ventured out into a common snowmobiling area at Logan Peak. They were riding near the summit of the east face of Logan Peak at the head of Providence Canyon, in an area known by local snowmobilers as the Rodeo Grounds, when an avalanche was triggered, catching and killing two of the snowmobilers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in the western Uinta Mountains caught and killed one snowmobiler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A father and his three sons left the Bear River Service trailhead in the Western Uinta Mountains for a day of snowmobiling on the morning of December 30th. They eventually made their way to Yamaha Hill (which Utah riders call Avalanche Hill), where one of the sons triggered a large avalanche. He got carried by the avalanche and was killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving just north and east of Utah brought strong winds to northern Utah, particularly along the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were recorded over 25 hours at the Chepeta RAWS, with the highest gust being 93 mph.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving just north and east of Utah brought strong winds to northern Utah, particularly along the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lakeside Mountain sensor recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty easterly canyon winds developed early in the morning along the Wasatch Front and in the Cache Valley in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sensor at the Mouth of Parley's Canyon recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty easterly canyon winds developed early in the morning along the Wasatch Front and in the Cache Valley in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage due to gusty winds was reported one mile east of Logan, where a 15 foot boat and a trailer were both flipped over into a yard. Also, a screen door was ripped off a house, and ten inch diameter tree branches were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in-bounds at the Snowbird ski resort resulted in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following the snow storm the previous day, the Mt. Baldy side of Snowbird resort was open to the public for the first time in the season. Prior to the avalanche, over a period of about 3 hours, an estimated 300 people crossed the starting zone of the avalanche or skied over the slope that eventually slid. At 12:24, a male snowboarder was on the high traverse of Northwest Mt. Baldy and initiated the avalanche. A 27-year-old female, who was hiking up the slope to retrieve a ski, got caught in the avalanche and was buried; she eventually died in the hospital due to injuries sustained from the avalanche.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet storm system brought widespread snowfall to basically the entire state of Utah, with the most significant accumulations occurring in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Harmony received a storm total of 22 inches of snow for the event, while Pintura received 13 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet storm system brought widespread snowfall to basically the entire state of Utah, with the most significant accumulations occurring in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mount Carmel Junction received a storm total of 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet storm system brought widespread snowfall to basically the entire state of Utah, with the most significant accumulations occurring in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kolob SNOTEL received 2.00 inches of liquid equivalent, or approximately 20 inches of new snow for the event. Long Valley Junction also received 1.40 liquid equivalent, much of which fell in a 12-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system tracked across the Four Corners area and producing significant snowfall across the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches occurred while wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and associated cold front swept across eastern Utah and brought significant snowfall and strong winds to the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 12 inches occurred while wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and associated cold front swept across eastern Utah and brought significant snowfall and strong winds to the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 6 inches occurred while wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and associated cold front swept across eastern Utah and brought significant snowfall and strong winds to the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 12 inches occurred while wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused an injury in Richfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was injured at the Cove View Golf Course in Richfield when lightning hit a nearby tree and traveled to her. The woman was conscious and breathing when she was taken to a nearby hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in portions of Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in the Capitol Gorge and Grand Wash areas of Capitol Reef National Park. This flooding closed these two areas, as well as the Scenic Drive in the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms with severe gusty winds were observed on July 19th and 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milford ASOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 59 mph due to a nearby thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms with severe gusty winds were observed on July 19th and 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media relayed a report of a sensor measuring a maximum wind gust of 65 mph due to a thunderstorm passing over the town of Hinckley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms with severe gusty winds were observed on July 19th and 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Durand Road sensor in the Dugway mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 61 mph from a passing thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms with severe gusty winds were observed on July 19th and 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst caused damage in several towns in Duchesne County. The damage included 8 downed power poles, mostly by trees falling on the lines, a shed that blew over in Myton, a few homes and businesses that lost roofing material, 10 trees on the south side of Highway 40 that blew down and closed the highway for a couple hours, and a couple of fences that were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through Salt Lake Valley, causing strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm came off of the Oquirrh Mountains and moved into Herriman. Members of the public in Herriman reported a measured wind gust of 60 mph, as well as an estimated gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow transported deep subtropical moisture across eastern Utah as an upper level moved east across the Great Basin. This system was quickly followed by a strong cold front out of the northwest. The combination of the two systems brought heavy snow and strong winds to eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve to seventeen inches of snowfall occurred while frequent wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph buffeted the area. A local wind gust of 59 mph occurred at Dutch John.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow transported deep subtropical moisture across eastern Utah as an upper level moved east across the Great Basin. This system was quickly followed by a strong cold front out of the northwest. The combination of the two systems brought heavy snow and strong winds to eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of snow fell across the majority of the area while wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph buffeted the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow transported deep subtropical moisture across eastern Utah as an upper level moved east across the Great Basin. This system was quickly followed by a strong cold front out of the northwest. The combination of the two systems brought heavy snow and strong winds to eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell at elevations above 5000 feet, or generally north of the San Juan River including the town of Blanding. One inch of less of snowfall occurred south of the San Juan River and in the Hovenweep area. Wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph buffeted the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow transported deep subtropical moisture across eastern Utah as an upper level moved east across the Great Basin. This system was quickly followed by a strong cold front out of the northwest. The combination of the two systems brought heavy snow and strong winds to eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eleven inches of snow fell across the area with frequent wind gusts of 25 to 50 mph. A gust of 63 mph was recorded at the Wildhorse RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moved east across northern Arizona and New Mexico and produced significant snowfall across southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 3 inches of snow fell across the majority of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system produced significant snowfall across the mountain and high plateau areas of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system produced significant snowfall across the mountain and high plateau areas of eastern Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system brought another round of heavy snow to portions of northern and central Utah.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wendover AWOS recorded the strongest wind gust for the entire evening, a 75 mph gust caused by a thunderstorm passing over the border from Nevada.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought heavy snow to much of central and northern Utah, with warm advection precipitation beginning on the 31st of January. Note that this episode continued into February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved inland and impacted much of Utah throughout the day of February 25th. A band of valley rain and mountain snow developed along the cold front on the 25th, sweeping through most of the state by the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Canyon SNOTEL received 1.70 inches of snow water equivalent for the storm, or approximately 18 inches of snow in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough brought heavy snow to much of northern Utah on the 20th and 21st. The snow was heavy at times, particularly on the morning of the 21st.

The Salt Lake Airport received 6.1 inches of snowfall for the day, which was a new record for the date, breaking the old mark of 4.5 inches in 1953.

Strong south flow ahead of the system transported substantial moisture across eastern Utah resulting in strong gusty winds and widespread moderate to heavy snowfall across the central and southern mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances in moist westerly flow moved across eastern Utah resulting in significant snowfall and strong winds for much of the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occurred above 8000 feet by storms end while wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph buffeted the area. For a continuation on this event see the February 2008 Storm Data publication.

A colder and more potent system moved into the state on the 31st, finally moving the front out of the area, but not before impacting the morning commute on the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Utah on the morning of March 29th and stalled over the state, bringing areas of heavy snow. A colder and more potent system moved into the state on the 31st, finally moving the front out of the area, but not before impacting the morning commute on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm had a major impact on traveling conditions the night of the 30th and during rush hour on the morning of the 31st. Utah Highway Patrol reported 99 crashes in Salt Lake County during this period of time, including 24 accidents involving injuries and 24 slide-offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty south winds developed ahead of an approaching cold front, with the strongest winds across much of western and central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sites recorded wind gusts greater than 60 mph, with the highest gust being a 75 mph gust in Tooele. The winds knocked down power lines and trees as large as 12 inches in diameter in both the Tooele and Salt Lake valleys. Damages also included many fences that were blown down, whole sides of houses that were missing siding, shingles blown off, and portions of roofs missing. The county of Tooele submitted around $40,000 worth of damages, including damages to communications equipment and vehicles. The city of Tooele submitted about $50,000 worth of damage, including damages sustained at the golf course, cemetery, and various city buildings. The damage estimate also includes large amounts of private property damage, including roof repairs and reports of trees falling on vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought very heavy snow to both the mountains and valleys of much of the state of Utah. Most of the snow fell throughout the day on the 3rd of February, while some areas, particularly the higher terrain, received snow showers into the 4th. This system also brought very strong winds to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many places across the southern Wasatch Front received more than a foot of new snow from this event, including 20 inches at Provo Canyon, 16 inches in Draper, and 16 inches of snow in Alpine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system and associated cold front pushed through Utah beginning in the afternoon of February 13th. Heavy snow developed along and behind this front. Strong north winds also developed behind the front, resulting in white-out conditions in many locations. This event was particularly high impact in the southern Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impact-wise, the southern Wasatch Front was hit the hardest by this storm. Woodland Hills and Payson both received a foot of new snow for the storm, but the real story was the effect on afternoon and evening travel.  With the heavy snowfall, this area received the strongest winds for the state in a valley location, with the strongest recorded gusts being 70 mph in Alpine and 69 mph in Lehi. This led to white-out conditions and high impacts to the afternoon commute. Over 200 students were stranded at 3 elementary schools, and ten buses with 60 to 70 students each were stranded because of road closures or weather conditions, most later returning to their schools until parents could get them. Numerous roads were closed down, including U.S. Highway 6 in Utah county east of Spanish Fork, and all of Redwood Road in Utah county. Many motorists were forced to abandon their cars and come back on Valentine's Day, only to find them buried in snow drifts, some as high as 10 feet. Many of these motorists spent the night in gas stations and grocery stores, and reports indicated that these stranded people numbered in the hundreds. Accidents with this storm included a 12-car pileup at Redwood Road and Lehi Main in Saratoga Springs, two jack-knifed semitrailers blocking several northbound lanes of I-15 near Point of the Mountain, a seven-car accident near American Fork, and 16 vehicles involved in a massive pile-up on Highway 73 in Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system and associated cold front pushed through Utah beginning in the afternoon of February 13th. Heavy snow developed along and behind this front. Strong north winds also developed behind the front, resulting in white-out conditions in many locations. This event was particularly high impact in the southern Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bountiful Bench received 7 inches of new snow from this storm. In addition, the afternoon rush hour was highly impacted by the snowfall, which included 36 mph wind gusts recorded at the Ogden ASOS. Several sections of Interstate 15 in Box Elder, Weber and Davis counties were closed throughout portions of the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system and associated cold front pushed through Utah beginning in the afternoon of February 13th. Heavy snow developed along and behind this front. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The effects of long term drought continued to be felt across eastern Utah even though a number of weather systems produced significant precipitation across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 15th into the 16th. Ahead of this front, high winds and areas of blowing dust developed across much of the state, making travel treacherous, particularly on east-west thoroughfares.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front pushed through eastern Utah and produced heavy snow and strong winds across much of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front pushed through eastern Utah and produced heavy snow and strong winds across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 6 to 12 inches occurred, mainly across the  western portion of the area. Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph caused blowing and drifting snow, especially across the eastern portion of the area. Blizzard conditions were reported north and east of Vernal where snow drifts of 3 to 6 feet developed. Highway 40 from Vernal to the Colorado State Line was closed and Highway 191 from Vernal to Daggett County was closed for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the state of Utah to start out the month of March, bringing rain, snow, and strong winds both ahead of and behind the cold front.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the state of Utah to start out the month of March, bringing rain, snow, and strong winds both ahead of and behind the cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Utah on the morning of March 29th and stalled over the state, bringing areas of heavy snow. A colder and more potent system moved into the state on the 31st, finally moving the front out of the area, but not before impacting the morning commute on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm greatly impacted travel in Utah County, primarily during the morning of the 31st. More than 130 accidents or slide-offs were reported throughout Utah County, including 58 accidents on I-15 and 29 accidents in Orem. Only 5 accidents involving injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist low pressure system brought heavy snow and strong winds to eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 20 to 30 inches occurred while wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph buffeted the area. Snow drifts were up to 10 feet high and many roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances in moist westerly flow moved across eastern Utah resulting in significant snowfall and strong winds for much of the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event began January 31. Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occurred above the 8000 foot level. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph buffeted the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold late season storm system produced strong winds and significant snowfall across the mountains of northeast Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah beginning on April 30th. Ahead of this front there were isolated strong winds in southwest Utah, but most of the moisture was limited to northern Utah. Note that this episode began in April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of fronts in quick succession brought snow to much of Utah. The first cold front arrived in northern Utah late on the 4th and brought widespread snow to northern Utah and heavy rain and mountain snow to southwest Utah on the 5th. Another cold front brought snow to the entire region on the 6th, continuing in southern Utah into the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seeley Creek SNOTEL received about 18 inches storm total, including 12 inches in 12 hours on January 6th. Also, Signal Peak RAWS recorded a maximum wind gust of 79 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors recorded wind gusts greater than 75 mph. In addition, Solitude received 33 inches of snow and Alta UDOT received 29 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought very strong wind and heavy snow to much of Utah. The cold front brought poor visibilities in snow and blowing snow to the Wasatch Front, resulting in numerous road closures and traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near white-out conditions with 40 mph winds were experienced on I-15, making travel nearly impossible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought very strong wind and heavy snow to much of Utah. The cold front brought poor visibilities in snow and blowing snow to the Wasatch Front, resulting in numerous road closures and traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near white-out conditions were experienced on roads in the Salt Lake Valley, including Interstates 15 and 80. At the peak of the storm, Salt Lake City ASOS recorded 1/8 mile visibility and 45 mph wind gusts. Interstate 80 east of Salt Lake City was closed for approximately 5 hours. A 34-year-old woman was killed in a one-car accident near Grantsville; slick conditions caused the woman's vehicle to roll into a wash where she was pronounced dead at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought very strong wind and heavy snow to much of Utah. The cold front brought poor visibilities in snow and blowing snow to the Wasatch Front, resulting in numerous road closures and traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and wind gusts up to 50 mph highly impacted travel. Interstate 15 near Payson was closed in both directions by a large chain-reaction pileup accident which stretched for 3 miles and involved 40 vehicles including 20 semi-trucks. Twenty-seven people were transported to local hospitals. With Salt Lake and Utah counties combined, troopers responded to a total of 200 traffic accidents from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flooding and flash flooding across portions of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred around Bluff and covered a portion of Highway 163 about 3 miles east of Bluff. A spotter reported 0.53 inches of rainfall in 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds buffeted the area ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from the south shifted to the north northeast and increased in intensity which caused tree limb damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flooding and flash flooding across portions of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused debris and water to flow over Highway 43 between mile markers 2 and 3.  Several inches of water was also reported running across Highway 44. Big rocks also fell across roadways near Sheep Creek.  A lot of debris flowed into nearby canals, and the Sheep Creek irrigation canal overflowed.  A dirt road to Sols Canyon was washed out, as well as E. 3rd Road in Manila, and a settling pond was also washed out.  Flooding also damaged several fields of crops, fences, landscaping, and irrigation systems.  Water and mud entered a church basement and garages of some residences.  Hail also caused crop damage.  Rainfall of 2 inches in 1 hour was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued to impact extreme northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate Drought conditions continued across extreme northern Daggett County at the beginning of May. However, the combination of significant winter precipitation and cool spring weather resulted in decreased dryness and an end of the drought conditions by month's end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cool and dry airmass allowed temperatures to fall below freezing across much of the lower valleys where the growing season had already begun.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures reached 27 to 32 degrees over more than half the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cool and dry airmass allowed temperatures to fall below freezing across much of the lower valleys where the growing season had already begun.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures reached 23 to 32 degrees over more than half the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cool and dry airmass allowed temperatures to fall below freezing across much of the lower valleys where the growing season had already begun.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures reached 29 to 32 degrees over about half the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms, some with very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on the south side of the Uinta Mountains produced widespread flash flooding across a large portion of Uintah County which extended from northwest to southwest of Vernal.  Highway 40 southwest of Vernal was closed for a while due to flood waters across the road. Many other roads in the impacted area were covered extensively with debris, up to four feet deep in places.  A portion of the Red Cloud Loop Road was washed out, with water flowing up to 10 feet deep and 15 feet wide.   Raging water came down the streets of the Flake subdivision west of Maeser.  Five homes had water in their basements and some had water in their garages, although most homes in the Flake subdivision are built up above the road.  There was extensive damage to fences, trees, sheds, equipment, and landscaping in that subdivision.  Debris also filled the sewer lines in the Maeser area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of moisture that reached the entire state of Utah helped initiate thunderstorms across a few different areas from July 20th to July 22nd. These thunderstorms mostly produced flash flooding, but large hail and strong winds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a flash flood of Virgin River near Bloomington, with a rise from 29 cfs to 1129 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of moisture that reached the entire state of Utah helped initiate thunderstorms across a few different areas from July 20th to July 22nd. These thunderstorms mostly produced flash flooding, but large hail and strong winds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scenic Drive in Capitol Reef National Park was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of moisture that reached the entire state of Utah helped initiate thunderstorms across a few different areas from July 20th to July 22nd. These thunderstorms mostly produced flash flooding, but large hail and strong winds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail that was 0.75 inches in diameter was observed in Kearns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of moisture that reached the entire state of Utah helped initiate thunderstorms across a few different areas from July 20th to July 22nd. These thunderstorms mostly produced flash flooding, but large hail and strong winds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported a rainfall total of 1.26 inches near Kearns. Local media reported flooding in the Kearns and Taylorsville area, and standing water accumulated 1 to 3 feet in low lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of moisture that reached the entire state of Utah helped initiate thunderstorms across a few different areas from July 20th to July 22nd. These thunderstorms mostly produced flash flooding, but large hail and strong winds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fort Pierce Wash rose to 1097 cfs within one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of thunderstorms toward the end of July caused flash flooding in a few different areas and produced some strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of streets and low lying areas in the town of Circleville, as well as flooding of several houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant moisture surge in mid-July aided in producing several flash flood events in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zion Park Rangers reported that two hikers were stranded along Taylor Creek in the Kolob Canyon section of the park due to a small flash flood. Radar estimated 1.0 to 1.5 inches of rain in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant moisture surge in mid-July aided in producing several flash flood events in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused the North Fork of the Virgin to rise from 42 to 143 cfs, and nearby campgrounds were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant moisture surge in mid-July aided in producing several flash flood events in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in the town of Ivins, damaging one home. In addition, numerous roads were closed due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant moisture surge in mid-July aided in producing several flash flood events in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zion Park Rangers observed a flash flood at the headwater areas of the Narrows region of the North Fork of the Virgin River. A rise from 50 cfs to 558 cfs was measured at the North Fork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season storm system brought cold and unstable air to eastern Utah that resulted in significant snowfall and strong gusty winds across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 13 inches occurred above 8000 feet while wind gusts of 20 to 25 mph produced blowing and drifting snow. A Special Avalanche Advisory was issued for the mountains of northern Utah due to the heavy late season snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning destroyed a transducer in Daggett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and destroyed a transducer at the Red Canyon Water Treatment Plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season storm system brought cold and unstable air to eastern Utah that resulted in significant snowfall and strong gusty winds across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 13 inches occurred above the 7000 foot level while wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued to impact extreme northeast Utah through the month. Elsewhere, significant winter precipitation and cool spring weather resulted in decreased dryness and an end of the drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate Drought conditions diminished to a very small area of northwest Daggett County by the end of the month. For a continuation on this drought situation please see the May 2008 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in south central Utah, creating multiple flash floods in Kane County over a three-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused large scale flash flooding along the Paria River drainage, including the Wawheap and Big Water areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in south central Utah, creating multiple flash floods in Kane County over a three-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Back country rangers reported a flash flood on a road along Last Chance Creek, which flows into Lake Powell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass allowed for widespread thunderstorm activity over a three-day period, which resulted in multiple flash floods across much of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flooding across SR-24 near Caineville due to heavy rain on Caineville Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in south central Utah, creating multiple flash floods in Kane County over a three-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional flooding in the lower Paria River drainage closed the Cottonwood Canyon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms across the state of Utah produced strong gusty winds in northern Utah and flash flooding in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred across much of Kane County. Flooding impacted the Cottonwood Canyon Road, as well as multiple other roads throughout the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in northern Utah produced severe hail in multiple locations over a two-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported 1.00 inch hail in Herriman. In addition, an airline pilot near Riverton reported nickel size hail (0.88 inches in diameter) fifteen minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in northern Utah produced severe hail in multiple locations over a two-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail (0.88 inches in diameter) fell near East Millcreek, but the NWS employee at the location reported that the hail fell apart as it hit the ground. In addition, hailstones with a diameter of 1.00 inch were reported by a member of the public in a similar location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass allowed for widespread thunderstorm activity over a three-day period, which resulted in multiple flash floods across much of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers in Zion National Park reported flooding in Hop Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass allowed for widespread thunderstorm activity over a three-day period, which resulted in multiple flash floods across much of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in Croton Canyon above Lake Powell produced a large flash flood that ran over a dirt road in area. Glen Canyon dispatch received reports from boaters of large amounts of debris from the drainage flowing into Lake Powell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced flash flooding in Washington County in far southwest Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused flash flooding in Snow Creek Canyon. This drainage eventually flows into the Santa Clara River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced flash flooding in Washington County in far southwest Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a large flash flood on North Creek flowing out of Zion National Park. Flows increased on the Virgin River, north of the town of Virgin, from 54 cfs to 2562 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass allowed for widespread thunderstorm activity over a three-day period, which resulted in multiple flash floods across much of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood occurred in Quail Creek, above I-15 near Hurricane. Rainfall amounts estimated by radar were as high as 2.9 inches in many areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass allowed for widespread thunderstorm activity over a three-day period, which resulted in multiple flash floods across much of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the town of New Harmony. Radar estimates of rainfall over the area averaged around 4 inches for the day, with estimates as high as 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of thunderstorms toward the end of July caused flash flooding in a few different areas and produced some strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Sage sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 65 mph. The Interstate 80 sensor also recorded a wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of thunderstorms toward the end of July caused flash flooding in a few different areas and produced some strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood occurred at Alvey Wash near Escalante due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific system moved slowly across the state, bringing the first major snowfall of the season to the valleys of northern Utah. Gusty winds also occurred ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Salt Lake received 7 inches of new snow, and Olympus Cove received 6.5 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific system moved slowly across the state, bringing the first major snowfall of the season to the valleys of northern Utah. Gusty winds also occurred ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roy and West Haven received 11 inches of new snow for the event, while South Ogden received 8 new inches. Heavy snow and winds knocked down many tree branches and a few small trees in Roy, Bountiful, and surrounding areas, including branches that blocked 5600 South in Roy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked near the Four Corners and brought significant snowfall to the southern and central mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to twelve inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked near the Four Corners and brought significant snowfall to the southern and central mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold airmass over the area caused the first widespread freezing temperatures of the season to occur in most of the lower valleys of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures of 24 to 32 degrees occurred across most of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold airmass brought the first widesread freezing temperatures to the extreme east-central Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures of 28 to 32 degrees occurred across most of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought widespread rain to the state of Utah, with heavy snow occurring at 9,000 feet above sea level and higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pickle Keg SNOTEL, located at 9,600 feet, received 1.50 inches of precipitation, including 1.00 inch in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought widespread rain to the state of Utah, with heavy snow occurring at 9,000 feet above sea level and higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kolob SNOTEL, at 9,250 feet elevation, received 1.40 inches of precipitation, including 1.00 inch in about 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific system moved slowly across the state, bringing the first major snowfall of the season to the valleys of northern Utah. Gusty winds also occurred ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flattop Mountain sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 60 mph ahead of a cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought widespread rain to the state of Utah, with heavy snow occurring at 9,000 feet above sea level and higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ben Lomond SNOTEL received 3.90 inches of precipitation over the two day period, with 3.00 inches of that falling in about 9 hours. This particular site received mostly liquid precipitation, but higher elevation sites received mostly snow, with similar snow water equivalent amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought widespread rain to the state of Utah, with heavy snow occurring at 9,000 feet above sea level and higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Snowbird SNOTEL received 1.50 inches of liquid precipitation, including 1.00 inch in 12 hours. Sites above 9,000 feet received all snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought widespread rain to the state of Utah, with heavy snow occurring at 9,000 feet above sea level and higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Canyon SNOTEL, located at 9,100 feet elevation, received 1.00 inch of precipitation in about 9 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in a very narrow slot canyon in the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in a small, but popular, slot canyon called Egypt 3, located near the Escalante River in the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area. This flooding resulted in two fatalities and two more injuries. ||The drainage of Egypt 3 is very small, measuring approximately 1.2 square miles. The area is void of vegetation and soil, and the underlying parent matter is Navajo Sandstone, which is virtually impermeable. Radar rainfall estimates indicated that approximately 0.2 to 0.4 inches of rain fell on the drainage. Based on calculations after the event, the peak discharge was estimated to be approximately 450 cfs, and the time from the beginning of the rainfall to peak discharge was estimated to be 24 minutes. With the dimensions of the channel, it is probable that the depth of the flood exceeded 20 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold airmass over the area caused the first widespread freezing temperatures of the season to occur in most of the lower valleys of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures of 27 to 30 degrees occurred across most of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold airmass over the area caused the first widespread freezing temperatures of the season to occur in most of the lower valleys of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures of 24 to 32 degrees occurred across most of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought early season snowfall to the higher mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early season snowfall of 3 to 8 inches fell above 9000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought early season snowfall to the higher mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early season snowfall of 3 to 6 inches fell above 9500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms across the state of Utah produced strong gusty winds in northern Utah and flash flooding in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gunnison Island mesonet site measured a maximum wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass allowed for widespread thunderstorm activity over a three-day period, which resulted in multiple flash floods across much of southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rapid and very large river rise on the Dirty Devil River occurred on the morning of August 8th, topping out at 8400 cfs.  This flood was due to heavy rainfall the day before in the Little Wild Horse Canyon area in the San Rafael Swell.
